name: Can my boss fire me if I’m physically unable to come back to work?
description: While employers generally cannot fire you for filing a claim, they are not always required to hold your job open indefinitely if you cannot perform your duties.

mainEntity.name: Can my boss fire me if I’m physically unable to come back to work?

acceptedAnswer.text: While employers generally cannot fire you for filing a claim, they are not always required to hold your job open indefinitely if you cannot perform your duties. Protections vary based on the ADA and state-specific retaliation laws.
acceptedAnswer.description:
In Utah and Idaho, an employer is generally not required to hold your specific position open indefinitely while you are on workers' comp. However, they cannot fire you solely in retaliation for filing a claim or because you were injured. If your injury results in a permanent disability that prevents you from doing your old job, the employer may eventually terminate your employment if no reasonable accommodation exists.

Eligibility for continued benefits depends on whether your 'medical stability' has been reached and if you have permanent restrictions.

- Protections under the ADA (Americans with Disabilities Act) may apply.
- State-specific 'at-will' employment laws influence termination rights.
- Retaliatory discharge is illegal and can lead to a separate lawsuit.

You should document all conversations regarding your job status and return-to-work efforts. If you are fired while on restrictions, legal intervention is often necessary to protect your secondary wage loss claims.
